map.condition: Edges already sorted; need MST time complexity
Dense graph, adjacency matrix; Prim's complexity
Sparse graph, binary min-heap; Prim's complexity

map.use: \text{MST is unique} \iff \text{all edge weights are distinct}

formulas.name: Minimum Spanning Tree Definition
formulas.latex: T \subseteq E,\; |T| = n-1,\; T \text{ is a spanning tree},\; w(T) = \min \sum_{e \in T} w(e)
formulas.plain: T is a subset of E with n-1 edges forming a spanning tree with minimum total weight.
formulas.whenToUse: Establishing what a valid MST is: connected, acyclic, spans all n vertices, n-1 edges.
formulas.trap: An MST always has exactly n-1 edges — confusing n (vertices) with m (edges) leads to wrong answers.

formulas.name: MST Edge Count
formulas.plain: MST has exactly n-1 edges for a graph with n vertices.
formulas.whenToUse: Verifying or counting edges in an MST.

terms.means: number of edges in the MST

formulas.trap: For a graph with multiple connected components, each component has its own MST (minimum spanning forest).

formulas.name: MST Uniqueness Condition
formulas.latex: \text{MST is unique} \iff \text{all edge weights are distinct}
formulas.plain: MST is unique if and only if all edge weights are distinct.
formulas.whenToUse: Answering how many MSTs a graph has when weights may be equal.
formulas.trap: Equal-weight edges can produce multiple MSTs; the number of MSTs is NOT simply the number of equal-edge subsets — requires careful enumeration.

formulas.name: Cut Property (Safe Edge Rule)
formulas.latex: \forall \text{ cut } (S, V\setminus S),\; e^* = \arg\min_{e \text{ crosses cut}} w(e) \Rightarrow e^* \in \text{some MST}
formulas.plain: The minimum weight edge crossing any cut of the graph belongs to some MST.
formulas.whenToUse: Proving correctness of greedy MST algorithms; justifying why a chosen edge is safe.
formulas.trap: The cut property gives membership in SOME MST, not all MSTs, when weights are not distinct.

formulas.name: Cycle Property
formulas.latex: \forall \text{ cycle } C,\; e_{\max} = \arg\max_{e \in C} w(e) \Rightarrow e_{\max} \notin \text{any MST (if unique max)}
formulas.plain: The unique maximum weight edge in any cycle does not belong to any MST.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying edges that can be excluded from the MST.
formulas.trap: If the max edge weight is not unique within the cycle, it may or may not appear in an MST.

formulas.name: Kruskal's Time Complexity (Unsorted Edges)
formulas.plain: Kruskal's algorithm runs in O(m log m) = O(m log n) when edges need to be sorted.
formulas.whenToUse: Finding MST with Kruskal when edges are not pre-sorted.
formulas.trap: O(m log m) and O(m log n) are the same because m <= n^2 so log m <= 2 log n — both are correct answers.

formulas.name: Kruskal's Time Complexity (Pre-sorted Edges)
formulas.plain: If edges are already sorted, Kruskal runs in O(m * alpha(n)) using Union-Find.
formulas.whenToUse: When the question states edges are already sorted and asks for Kruskal's complexity.
formulas.trap: Often approximated as O(m) or O(n) but strictly O(m * alpha(n)); GATE may accept O(m) for sorted case.

formulas.name: Kruskal's Algorithm Steps
formulas.latex: \text{Sort edges by weight} \to \text{For each edge: if no cycle (Union-Find), add to MST}
formulas.plain: Sort edges ascending; greedily add each edge if it does not form a cycle, using Union-Find to detect cycles.
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing Kruskal's execution or verifying which edges are selected.

terms.means: disjoint set union data structure to detect cycles

formulas.trap: Kruskal processes edges globally in sorted order — not vertex by vertex like Prim's.

formulas.name: Prim's Time Complexity (Adjacency Matrix)
formulas.plain: Prim's algorithm with adjacency matrix runs in O(n^2).
formulas.whenToUse: Dense graphs represented as adjacency matrix.
formulas.trap: For dense graphs (m ~ n^2), Prim with matrix is better than Kruskal; for sparse graphs, Kruskal or Prim with heap is better.

formulas.name: Prim's Time Complexity (Binary Min-Heap + Adjacency List)
formulas.plain: Prim's algorithm with a binary min-heap and adjacency list runs in O((m+n) log n).
formulas.whenToUse: Sparse graphs where adjacency list + priority queue is used.
formulas.trap: With a Fibonacci heap this improves to O(m + n log n) — GATE sometimes distinguishes these.

formulas.name: Prim's Time Complexity (Fibonacci Heap)
formulas.plain: Prim's algorithm with a Fibonacci heap runs in O(m + n log n).
formulas.whenToUse: Theoretical best complexity of Prim's; used when comparing data structures.
formulas.trap: Fibonacci heap has high constant factors in practice; preferred theoretically but rarely implemented.

formulas.name: Prim's Algorithm Steps
formulas.latex: \text{Start at any vertex} \to \text{Repeatedly add min-weight edge from } V_{\text{visited}} \text{ to } V_{\text{unvisited}}
formulas.plain: Start from any vertex; repeatedly pick the minimum weight edge connecting visited to unvisited vertices until all vertices are included.
formulas.whenToUse: Tracing Prim's execution or verifying the sequence of edges selected.

terms.means: set of vertices already in the MST
set of vertices not yet in the MST

formulas.trap: Prim grows the MST vertex by vertex from a single source — not globally sorted like Kruskal; starting vertex affects traversal order but not the final MST weight.

formulas.name: All MSTs Have Equal Weight
formulas.latex: w(T_1) = w(T_2) \text{ for any two MSTs } T_1, T_2 \text{ of the same graph}
formulas.plain: All MSTs of a given graph have the same total weight, even if the edge sets differ.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether different MSTs can have different total weights.

terms.means: total weight of spanning tree T

formulas.trap: Different MSTs can have different edge sets but always the same total weight — do not confuse edge uniqueness with weight uniqueness.

formulas.name: Number of Spanning Trees in Complete Graph
formulas.latex: \text{# spanning trees of } K_n = n^{n-2} \quad (\text{Cayley's formula})
formulas.plain: The complete graph K_n has n^(n-2) distinct spanning trees (Cayley's formula).
formulas.whenToUse: Counting spanning trees of a complete graph; MSTs among them depend on weights.
formulas.trap: Cayley's formula counts ALL spanning trees, not just minimum ones. With all equal weights, every spanning tree is an MST.

formulas.name: MSTs with Equal-Weight Edges
formulas.latex: \text{If all edges have equal weight } w,\; w(\text{MST}) = (n-1) \cdot w
formulas.plain: If all edge weights are equal, every spanning tree is an MST with weight (n-1)*w.
formulas.whenToUse: When graph has all edges with the same weight and question asks for number of MSTs.

terms.means: number of edges in spanning tree

formulas.trap: With all equal weights, the number of MSTs equals the total number of spanning trees, which can be exponential.

formulas.name: Kruskal vs Prim Comparison
formulas.latex: \text{Kruskal: } O(m \log m) \text{ (best for sparse)} \quad \text{Prim: } O(n^2) \text{ or } O((m+n)\log n) \text{ (best for dense)}
formulas.plain: Kruskal is O(m log m) — better for sparse graphs. Prim is O(n^2) with matrix — better for dense graphs.
formulas.whenToUse: Choosing which algorithm is more efficient for a given graph density.
formulas.trap: For dense graphs where m ~ n^2, Kruskal's O(m log m) ~ O(n^2 log n) is worse than Prim's O(n^2).

formulas.name: Greedy Correctness of MST Algorithms
formulas.latex: \text{Both Kruskal and Prim are correct} \iff \text{Cut Property holds (matroid structure)}
formulas.plain: Both algorithms are provably correct via the cut property and the graphic matroid structure.
formulas.whenToUse: Justifying why greedy works for MST but not all optimization problems.
formulas.trap: Greedy does NOT give optimal solutions in general — MST is a special case where matroid structure guarantees optimality.

formulas.name: MST with Negative Weights
formulas.latex: \text{MST algorithms work correctly with negative edge weights}
formulas.plain: Both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms correctly find the MST even when edge weights are negative.
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether MST algorithms handle negative weights.
formulas.trap: Unlike shortest path algorithms (Bellman-Ford needed for negatives), MST algorithms handle negative weights without modification.
